stories.headline: Severe storms bring golf ball-sized hail, tornado warnings and flight delays to Denver metro
stories.summary:
Severe thunderstorms swept across the Denver metro area and northeastern Colorado on Monday, triggering multiple tornado warnings, reports of golf ball-sized hail and flash flooding. The National Weather Service issued damaging hail alerts for downtown Denver and surrounding communities, with the Storm Prediction Center placing the region under a Slight Risk for severe weather.

All flights in and out of Denver International Airport were grounded temporarily Monday afternoon as the storm system moved through, causing significant ground delays and stops. The Federal Aviation Administration confirmed the ground stop as hail and intense rain battered the airport and surrounding areas.

The storm system pushed east into northeastern Colorado after hitting the metro, leaving behind damaged vehicles and flooded roadways. Residents were urged to stay off roads and seek shelter during the most intense portions of the storm.

stories.headline: Federal judge blocks breakup of Boulder's NCAR, blasts Trump administration for political retaliation against Colorado
stories.summary:
A federal judge in Denver on Monday issued a 38-page injunction blocking federal officials from breaking up Boulder's National Center for Atmospheric Research by transferring its supercomputing center to the University of Wyoming. Senior U.S. District Judge R. Brooke Jackson ruled the National Science Foundation's divestiture attempt was 'arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, or otherwise not in accordance with law.'

Jackson accepted arguments from the University Corporation for Atmospheric Research that the Trump administration's actions constituted political retribution against Colorado, citing the state's Democratic voting history and the imprisonment of former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters. The judge noted that NCAR had already suffered a significant 'brain drain' of scientists, engineers and systems administrators as a direct result of the attempted transfer.

The injunction found that UCAR, which manages NCAR operations as a consortium of more than 100 universities, demonstrated irreparable harm and was likely to succeed in its lawsuit filed in March. The National Science Foundation declined to comment on the ruling. The decision comes on the same day Gov. Jared Polis ordered Tina Peters released from prison under a clemency order, a move that angered many Colorado Democrats.

stories.headline: Denver City Councilwoman Sarah Parady announces August resignation due to health concerns
stories.summary:
Denver City Councilwoman Sarah Parady announced Monday during a council meeting that she will resign in August due to undisclosed health problems, drawing a standing ovation from fellow council members and those in attendance. Parady, elected as an at-large council member in 2023, said through tears that going up stairs had become more exhausting than climbing a 14er and that she was experiencing spinning spells and pain throughout her body.

Parady said she believes her condition is a chronic illness rather than a progressive or undiagnosed disease, but that she cannot simultaneously fulfill her council duties, be a parent and pursue medical answers. She will step down on Aug. 5 specifically to avoid triggering the cost of a special election; her successor will instead be chosen on the November ballot and will serve the remainder of her term through July 19, 2027.

Parady, who is also an attorney, was known for detailed scrutiny of mayoral initiatives, including opposition to the city's Flock license plate reader system and questions about Mayor Mike Johnston's plan to purchase land for a women's soccer stadium. Johnston praised her in a statement as a 'tireless advocate for Denverites,' while Council President Amanda Sandoval called her a 'thoughtful leader and principled voice.' She will remain on the council until her resignation date but will no longer chair the Community Planning and Housing Committee.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis vetoes social media safety bill born from Evergreen High School shooting
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis vetoed House Bill 26-1255 on Thursday, legislation that would have required social media companies to notify law enforcement when they restrict or suspend user accounts over threatening posts. The bill was created in direct response to the September 2025 shooting at Evergreen High School, in which a student shot and injured two classmates before turning the gun on himself.

In his veto letter, Polis cited 'considerable concerns' about the bill's ambiguity, saying it would create incentives for companies to turn over large volumes of records to law enforcement even when no law had been violated, effectively stifling free speech on online forums. He pointed instead to a separate bill he signed in March requiring social networks to comply with warrants within 72 hours.

Evergreen parent Kim Halligan and bill sponsor Rep. Tammy Story both expressed disappointment, with Halligan saying police reports showed warning signs before the shooting that existing systems were not equipped to act upon in time. Story and Halligan pledged to reintroduce similar legislation in the next session under new legislative leadership.

stories.headline: Democrats Danielson and Gonzalez clash on election security, Trump threats in secretary of state primary
stories.summary:
Two elected officials from the Denver suburbs will face off in Colorado's Democratic primary for secretary of state on June 30: state Sen. Jessie Danielson of Wheat Ridge and Amanda Gonzalez, the Jefferson County clerk and recorder. The race opened after incumbent Jena Griswold, who is term-limited, announced she is running for attorney general.

The Colorado Sun interviewed both candidates on key issues including election security and resistance to federal interference. Danielson, a state senator since 2019, emphasized 'Trump-proofing' Colorado's elections and communicating voter rights clearly amid federal misinformation. Gonzalez, who has led Common Cause and currently runs Jefferson County elections, stressed transparency, legal action as a tool and countering voter suppression through accurate public information.

The Republican nominee is James Wiley, who faces no primary competition. The secretary of state race has gained heightened attention amid ongoing federal tensions over election administration and mail-ballot access nationally.

stories.headline: Colorado becomes first state to allow damages lawsuits for conversion therapy survivors
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed the Civil Actions for Conversion Therapy Survivors bill into law, allowing Coloradans harmed by 'sexual orientation or gender identity change efforts' to sue for damages under the state's medical malpractice statutes. The Democrat-sponsored legislation also removes the two-year statute of limitations on such claims, giving survivors a broader window to seek legal recourse.

The measure positions Colorado as the first state to create a civil cause of action specifically for conversion therapy survivors. Conversion therapy, widely discredited by major medical and psychological associations, refers to attempts to change a person's sexual orientation or gender identity through psychological or spiritual interventions.

The bill's passage comes amid a national legal backdrop in which the U.S. Supreme Court has weighed related questions about regulation of speech in therapeutic settings. Supporters say the law provides meaningful accountability for practitioners who cause harm to LGBTQ individuals.

stories.headline: Trial opens for Jefferson County man accused of fatally shooting teenager who knocked on barn door
stories.summary:
A trial opened Monday for a Jefferson County man charged with shooting a teenager who knocked on his barn door, in a case that drew significant public attention when it was first reported. The defendant faces charges related to the death of the young victim, whose family says the encounter was an innocent mistake.

Details of the defense and prosecution opening arguments were not immediately available from the candidate text, but the case has raised broader questions about self-defense laws and the rights of property owners in Colorado. Jefferson County prosecutors are expected to argue the shooting was not legally justified.

The trial is being closely watched as Colorado courts continue to grapple with questions about use-of-force statutes. No verdict has been reached.

stories.headline: Man killed in Aurora hit-and-run after collision with stolen motorcycle on E. Iliff Avenue
stories.summary:
An adult male motorcyclist died Friday night after a hit-and-run crash involving a stolen motorcycle at the intersection of East Iliff Avenue and South Idalia Street in Aurora, according to Aurora Police Department. A female passenger on the motorcycle was taken to a hospital in stable condition.

Police say the motorcycle was traveling westbound on East Iliff when it collided with a Honda CR-V that was turning onto South Idalia Street from eastbound East Iliff. The CR-V driver fled the scene; the vehicle was later found abandoned nearby, but the driver has not been located.

Investigators say speed may have been a contributing factor. The identity of the deceased has not been released, and the crash remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Hotter, wetter summer forecast for Colorado raises both drought relief and wildfire concerns
stories.summary:
A seasonal outlook from federal forecasters projects a hotter and wetter-than-average summer across Colorado in 2026, with the elevated precipitation pattern expected to bring some relief to drought-stressed areas along the Front Range and Denver metro. The increased moisture could help grasses and trees recover from recent dry conditions.

However, climate experts caution that above-normal precipitation also carries wildfire risk by promoting rapid growth of fine fuels such as grasses on the eastern plains and in communities near Boulder County. Should a dry stretch follow a wet period, the overgrown vegetation becomes highly combustible.

The forecast is relevant for water managers, emergency planners and outdoor enthusiasts across the state as the summer season begins. Officials have not yet issued specific drought or fire preparedness advisories tied to the seasonal outlook.

stories.headline: Denver office market divided: Cherry Creek thrives while downtown vacancy hits 38.9 percent
stories.summary:
Denver's office real estate market is showing sharp contrasts between submarkets, with Cherry Creek posting some of the country's best performance while downtown Denver struggles with a vacancy rate of 38.9 percent, according to an analysis presented at a NAIOP event. Industry experts say the divergence reflects a broader national trend of tenants seeking newer, amenity-rich office environments in walkable neighborhoods.

New investments in downtown Denver, including the planned $3 million upgrade of Denver Pavilions announced last week, signal that stakeholders have not abandoned the city core. However, analysts say a meaningful recovery in downtown office occupancy will likely take years given the current remote and hybrid work environment.

The bifurcated performance has significant implications for Denver's property tax base and the economic vitality of the urban core, which depends on office workers patronizing restaurants, retail and entertainment venues.

stories.headline: Dick's Sporting Goods to open 90,000-square-foot House of Sport at Larkridge in Denver metro
stories.summary:
Dick's Sporting Goods plans to open an immersive, 90,000-square-foot House of Sport location at the Larkridge shopping center in the Denver metro area, according to the Denver Business Journal. The format is designed as an interactive retail experience featuring golf simulators, batting cages and a climbing wall, among other amenities.

House of Sport is Dick's highest-end retail concept, with a limited number of locations nationally. The Larkridge opening would mark the format's debut in the Denver metro market, giving the region a flagship-style sporting goods destination.

The development is a positive signal for the Larkridge center, which like many suburban retail properties has sought to attract experiential tenants to drive foot traffic. A specific opening date was not disclosed.

stories.headline: Avalanche sign Russian goaltender Nikita Novosyolov to two-year entry-level contract
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Monday the signing of goaltender Nikita Novosyolov to a two-year entry-level contract beginning with the 2026-27 season. Novosyolov, 21, spent the majority of the past season with Gornyak-UGMK in Russia's VHL, posting a 22-10-8 record, a 2.10 goals-against average and a .932 save percentage in 40 games, ranking in the league's top 10 in both wins and save percentage.

The 6-foot-2 netminder from Krasnoturinsk, Russia, also made three appearances in the KHL for Avtomobilist Yekaterinburg this past season, his first games at Eurasia's highest professional level. Novosyolov earned VHL Rookie of the Month honors in March 2025 and has been teammates with Avalanche prospect Nikita Ishimnikov in the Avtomobilist system since 2022-23.

The signing adds depth to Colorado's goaltending pipeline as the franchise looks to build toward another Stanley Cup run. Novosyolov is expected to develop in the AHL before competing for an NHL roster spot.

joke.joke: You know how to tell when someone is a really good friend? They don't bring up that thing you did.

topStory.headline: Trump Reconsidering $1.8 Billion 'Anti-Weaponization' Fund as Court Order, Republican Opposition Mount
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump is reconsidering his push for a $1.776 billion compensation fund for what the administration has described as victims of politically motivated prosecutions, according to a person familiar with his thinking. The Justice Department has paused the program to comply with a temporary block issued Friday by U.S. District Judge Leonie Brinkema in Virginia, who halted any transfer of funds following a lawsuit filed by Democracy Forward. A federal hearing on the matter has been scheduled for June 12.

The fund, known as the Anti-Weaponization Fund, was announced last month as part of a settlement in Trump's lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service over the leak of his personal tax returns. Critics have pointed to the unusual nature of the settlement, noting that the IRS — whose leadership is appointed by the president — did not mount a defense against the suit, prompting accusations of collusion. The fund carries no requirement to disclose the identities of recipients or the amounts paid out, and administration officials have acknowledged that individuals pardoned for roles in the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot could qualify for payments.

Opposition has emerged from both parties. Senate Majority Leader John Thune told reporters the cleanest resolution would be for the administration to shut the fund down voluntarily. Former Vice President Mike Pence called the prospect of compensating rioters who assaulted police officers 'deeply offensive,' adding that the sentiment was broadly shared by Republicans and the general public. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, in a letter to colleagues, called the fund 'Trump's most brazen act of self-dealing yet' and pledged that Senate Democrats would force a floor vote to block payments.

An administration source told Axios the fund had 'become a distraction,' adding that while the president believes government was weaponized against people beyond himself, the fund was 'not the time and vehicle' to address it. Several Democratic-led states have moved to further complicate distributions: California Governor Gavin Newsom has proposed a 100 percent state income tax on any fund payments made to California residents, with similar proposals surfacing in Illinois, New York, and Connecticut.

The episode represents a rare instance of significant intraparty friction for the Trump White House, with prominent Republican figures publicly pressuring the administration to retreat on a presidential priority. Whether Trump formally abandons the fund or seeks an alternative mechanism will likely become clearer at or before the June 12 court hearing.
